# Data schemas (so you never need to open `tables/*.json`)

**Schema version 2.** All generated by `scripts/generate_tables.py`
(CoolProp 7.2.0). One directory per fluid key (`R134a`, `R744`, …) containing
`sat.json`, `superheat.json`, `subcool.json`, plus `tables/manifest.json`.
`tables.js` asserts `manifest.schema_version === 2` at init. Update this doc
and bump the version everywhere when the schema changes.

## tables/manifest.json

```json
{
  "schema_version": 2, "generated_utc": "...", "coolprop_version": "7.2.0",
  "fluids": {
    "R134a": { "files": [...], "T_crit_C": 101.06, "P_crit_kPa": 4059.3,
                "T_min_C": -50, "T_max_C": 100, "P_max_kPa": 5000 }
  }
}
```
14 fluids: R32, R1234yf, R1234ze, R454B, R452B, R134a, R410A, R407C, R404A,
R22, R290, R600a, R744, R717.

## sat.json — saturation table (indexed by T)

- `columns`: `["T","P_sat","hf","hg","sf","sg","rhof","rhog","uf","ug","P_bub","P_dew"]`
  (`P_sat` is mid-glide Q=0.5; `P_bub`/`P_dew` differ for zeotropic blends).
- `rows`: arrays in column order, T ascending in 0.5 °C steps (0.1 °C near
  critical for R744), ending at min(T_max, T_crit − 0.5).
- Units: °C, kPa, kJ/kg, kJ/kg·K, kg/m³ (same convention everywhere).

## superheat.json / subcool.json — ΔT-from-saturation grids

```json
{
  "fluid": "R134a", "type": "superheat", "schema_version": 2,
  "dT_values_K": [0, 2, 5, ...], "P_values_kPa": [50, 75, ...],
  "Tsat_C": [-40.45, ...],
  "properties": ["h", "s", "rho", "cp", "u"],
  "grid": { "h": [[...]], "s": [[...]], ... }
}
```
- `grid[prop][i][j]` = value at T = `Tsat_C[j]` ± `dT_values_K[i]`
  (+ for superheat above the dew line, − for subcool below the bubble line).
- `Tsat_C[j]` is the dew temperature (superheat) or bubble temperature
  (subcool) at `P_values_kPa[j]` — this is what makes glide handling correct.
- Grids are dense; the only `null`s are a physically unreachable corner for
  blends (deep subcool at vacuum pressures, where the mixture EOS has no data).
- P columns: standard set within [P_triple, 0.99·P_crit] plus near-critical
  anchors at {0.80, 0.90, 0.95, 0.99}·P_crit.
- `tables.js` interpolates **log-P** along the pressure axis, linear in ΔT.
- ΔT=0 rows are computed on the saturation curve itself (PQ flash), so the
  anchor row is exact.

## data/refrigerants.json — metadata (hand-maintained, editable)

Per fluid: `ashrae_designation, chemical_name, formula, molecular_weight,
safety_class, GWP_AR4, GWP_AR5, ODP, normal_boiling_point_C, T_crit_C,
P_crit_kPa, T_glide_C, blend_components, refrigerant_type, regulatory_status,
typical_applications[], replaces[], replaced_by[]`.
Keys here must match `tables/manifest.json` fluid keys exactly.

## Inspecting tables without reading them

```bash
python3 -c "import json; t=json.load(open('tables/R134a/superheat.json')); \
print(t['T_values_C'], t['P_values_kPa'])"
```

## Backend input pairs (`tables.js getProps`)

`TQ` (T °C, quality) · `PQ` (P kPa, quality) · `TP` (single-phase) ·
`PH` (P, h) · `PS` (P, s). Returns `{T_C, P_kPa, h, s, u, rho, cp, x}`;
`x` is `null` for single-phase states, `cp` is `null` in the two-phase region.
PH/PS return the independent variable (h or s) exactly. TQ pressure follows
the glide: P = P_bub + x·(P_dew − P_bub).
